one_line_takeaway: Cordon reframes agent tool-use safety as a systems/transactions problem -- wrapping each agent task in a semantic transaction with lineage tracking, shadow-state speculative execution, and a staged effect outbox -- and shows this task-level commit boundary catches all 45/45 cross-step policy violations in its benchmark versus only 14/45 for adapted per-call/sandbox/DLP-style defenses, at moderate latency/token overhead and near-zero cost to benign task correctness.
key_finding: On the 45-case correlated-risk suite: plain execution commits the risky/policy-violating effect in 45/45 cases; strategy adapters derived from existing defense boundaries intercept 14/45 before commit, miss 26/45 (unsafe), and detect 5/45 only post-hoc (after the effect has already committed); Cordon intercepts 45/45 before commit. On performance (Table 4, includes approval wait): mean task time is 25.55s (Plain) vs 31.35s (Approve-all, +22.7%), 23.64s (Reject-on-risk, -7.5%), 31.12s (Mixed, +21.8%); LLM calls fall from 162 (Plain) to 119-127 under transaction mediation; token use drops 23.6-28.4% and, with approval wait excluded, mean task time drops 24.6-27.9% relative to plain execution. On rollback (Table 5, deterministic trajectories, 15 trials): Cordon achieves 4.17ms median rollback latency, 178.95ms median recovery time, 0 residual filesystem deltas, and 15/15 resume checks passed, versus Git 'restore' (10.99ms rollback, 73 residual deltas, 0/15 resume passed), Git 'reset' (12.27ms, 73 residual deltas, 0/15 resume passed), and Git 'reset+clean' (21.74ms, 0 residual deltas, 12/15 resume passed). On benign benchmarks (Table 6): tau-bench 87.5% (Plain) vs 90.0% (Cordon, +2.5); Terminal-Bench 100.0% vs 100.0% (+0.0) -- i.e., no measurable correctness degradation from transaction mediation. Cost breakdown (Figure 9, excluding approval wait): LLM provider latency dominates end-to-end runtime at 62.3-63.6% across approve/reject/mixed modes; agent/tool execution is 14.2-14.4%; Cordon's transaction-control path is 22.2-23.4%; for deterministic recovery, the rollback primitive itself is only 2.9% of measured time.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Cordon, a transactional execution runtime for tool-using LLM agents that inserts a 'semantic transaction' boundary between the agent planner and side-effecting tools. Instead of treating each tool call as an isolated RPC (the dominant current design), Cordon groups an entire agent task into one transaction that tracks tool intents, a lineage graph over derived result objects, reversible local mutations executed speculatively in shadow state, and external effects staged in an 'effect outbox' rather than dispatched immediately. A validation engine evaluates this composed transaction (lineage + authority + staged effects) against a constraint tuple before allowing commit; on failure it rolls back shadow state and cancels staged effects. The authors formalize this with a small operational model (execution history H, dependency relation, commit predicate, nine 'transactional containment invariants' I1-I9 covering lineage flow, commit discipline, and authority/accountability) and build a working prototype (~14.4 KLOC runtime + 3.9 KLOC benchmark driver, mostly Python with a gRPC/protobuf IDL, ZODB FileStorage for transactional metadata, and command containment via a Rust-backed 'nono' sandbox).
threat_model: The threat model is cross-step / composed-flow risk in tool-using agents: individual tool calls look benign in isolation (e.g., reading logs, running a helper command, summarizing, sending a Slack message) but their composition produces a policy-violating irreversible effect (e.g., a secret read in one step flows through a derived summary into an external message; or a 'routine' helper command hides a write to .ssh/config; or a workspace-local delete command has a high-fanout deletion effect). The paper explicitly does not target a specific attacker (e.g., prompt injection is treated as one contributing risk category among several, not the sole focus) -- it targets the general class of 'cross-step semantic side effects' whose commit decision depends on evidence distributed across a dependency chain (Dep_H(s)) rather than any single tool call. The paper explicitly states its invariant suite 'is not a complete theory of agent safety' and that operations crossing unsupported/opaque channels (unsupported plugins, dynamically changing services, unobservable side effects) fall outside its containment scope.
limitations: The paper explicitly states: (1) the invariant suite (I1-I9) 'is not a complete theory of agent safety' -- it defines transaction-level commit-validity conditions, not universal containment; (2) guarantees apply only to operations within the mediated transaction runtime whose mutations/effects are observable to the system -- unsupported plugins, dynamically changing services, or tools with unobservable side effects fall outside containment scope, and for those Cordon only records lineage/authority/recovery metadata for audit/compensation rather than guaranteeing reversibility; (3) the shadow-filesystem design is application-level/userspace and materializes command views by copying files, so command startup cost is proportional to materialized workspace size (mitigated by excluding high-churn directories like .git, node_modules, __pycache__); (4) the engineering choices (JSON-envelope RPC, manifest-based shadow filesystem, ZODB object database) explicitly favor portability/observability over raw performance, and the authors state high-throughput distributed deployment would require a different storage backend; (5) evaluation is on a single commercial agent runtime ('Agent-H') with a single LLM (DeepSeek-V4-Pro), and the correlated-risk benchmark suite is authored by the paper's own team (built from patterns in existing benchmarks, not an independently validated public benchmark) rather than a third-party standard; (6) external effects that have crossed the boundary (dispatched) cannot be physically undone -- Cordon relies on idempotency keys and audit/compensation rather than true rollback for released effects.

datasets_used: Correlated-risk suite: 45 risk-bearing multi-tool workflows (paper-authored, cross product of 9 defense-boundary categories x 5 transaction-level risk families, across 6 agent domains: coding, incident response, document processing, office, customer support, data analysis)
tau-bench (Yao et al. 2024) -- benign task-completion benchmark
Terminal-Bench (Merrill et al. 2026) -- benign task-completion benchmark
quantitative_results: Pre-commit interception: Plain 0/45, strategy adapters 14/45 (26/45 unsafe, 5/45 post-hoc only), Cordon 45/45
Mean task time (incl. approval wait): Plain 25.55s, Approve-all 31.35s (+22.7%), Reject-on-risk 23.64s (-7.5%), Mixed 31.12s (+21.8%)
LLM calls: 162 (Plain) down to 119-127 under transaction mediation
Rollback: Cordon 4.17ms median rollback, 178.95ms median recovery, 0 residual deltas, 15/15 resume passed
Rollback baselines: Git restore 10.99ms/73 deltas/0-15 resume; Git reset 12.27ms/73 deltas/0-15 resume; Git reset+clean 21.74ms/0 deltas/12-15 resume

Runtime cost share (excl. approval wait): LLM provider latency 62.3-63.6%, agent/tool execution 14.2-14.4%, Cordon transaction-control path 22.2-23.4%; rollback primitive only 2.9% of deterministic-recovery time
Implementation size: ~14.4 KLOC runtime + 3.9 KLOC benchmark driver
baselines_compared: Human Approval (tool-level approval without scoped transaction context)
Sandbox Only (process/command containment, e.g. Docker seccomp, Inspect Sandboxing Toolkit)
Git restore / Git reset / Git reset+clean (rollback-specific baselines in Table 5/Figure 9)
